summary_text:
Chief Complaint
Left hand pain.

History of Present Illness
The patient reports pain in the left hand. The patient reports diarrhea related to eating pasta with a creamy sauce. The patient reports constipation. The patient reports experiencing constipation. The patient reports increased constipation. The patient reports diarrhea. The patient reports bouts of diarrhea. The patient reports hepatomegaly. The patient reports an enlarged liver. The doctor feels a slight enlargement of the liver on the upper right side. The doctor reports an enlarged liver. The patient's liver is enlarged. The doctor feels stool in the lower colon, which suggests constipation. The patient reports an enlarged liver. The patient had slightly elevated liver enzymes in a previous physical exam four months ago. The patient had blood drawn four months ago. The patient reports experiencing constipation. The patient reports increased constipation. The patient reports diarrhea related to eating pasta with a creamy sauce. The patient reports diarrhea. The patient reports bouts of diarrhea. The patient reports hepatomegaly. The patient reports an enlarged liver. The patient reports constipation. The patient reports diarrhea. The patient reports hepatomegaly. The patient reports an enlarged liver.

Findings
The lung sounds are clear. The heart sounds are normal. The patient's liver enzymes are slightly elevated.

Assessment
The patient has elevated liver enzymes. The doctor suspects liver issues. The patient's liver is enlarged.

Plan
The patient should refrain from drinking alcohol. The doctor recommends refraining from drinking alcohol. The doctor recommends discussing Crestor with the primary care physician. The patient should return for a follow-up appointment in four weeks. Order shoulder radiographs, initiate physical therapy, and continue acetaminophen as needed. Arrange follow-up in four weeks if symptoms persist.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Abdominal pain and diarrhea.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Patrick Allen is a 42-year-old male who presents for a new patient visit for chronic abdominal pain, constipation, and diarrhea.

Mr. Allen reports experiencing intermittent mid-abdominal pain for approximately 2 years. The pain is localized just inferior to the umbilicus and he describes it as a “nagging feeling” when it is present. The pain sometimes improves following a bowel movement. Unfortunately, Mr. Allen reports that the pain has been present more often in the past 2 months. In 01/2020, the patient presented to the emergency room due to the pain and underwent a CT scan.

In addition to the abdominal pain, Mr. Allen complains of constipation and episodic severe diarrhea for the past 2 months; however, he estimates that the constipation is more frequent than the diarrhea. When he is constipated, he has a bowel movement 3-4 times a week and the stools are hard. Regarding his diarrhea, he has noticed that it seems to be associated with certain foods. In particular, he notes that pasta with a creamy sauce is likely to prompt an episode of diarrhea. When this occurs, he usually has just 1 bowel movement of diarrhea and then it resolves. The patient states that, prior to 2 months ago, his bowel movements were normal. Mr. Allen notes that massaging his abdomen has sometimes been helpful in producing a bowel movement.

The patient’s last physical was 4 months ago, and he confirms that he did have bloodwork that day. Mr. Allen reports that his primary care provider notified him that his AST and ALT were mildly elevated and advised that they would need to recheck his liver enzymes in 6 months. The patient’s medication list includes Crestor and olmesartan daily and he estimates that he has been taking the Crestor for approximately 18 months. He also takes Tylenol as needed for pain, approximately once a month. The patient states that he drinks 2 beers once a week and denies a history of heavy alcohol or drug use. He also denies excessive bruising or bleeding and any lower extremity edema.

The patient denies blood in his stools, nausea, vomiting, heartburn, and indigestion. He confirms that he is eating and drinking normally, and his weight has been stable. He does acknowledge that he would like to lose 25 pounds, however. He has no family history of gastrointestinal cancer or liver disease; however, his sister has irritable bowel syndrome (IBS). The patient has not had any prior abdominal surgeries and he has never had a colonoscopy.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Respiratory
Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally.

Cardiovascular
No murmurs, gallops, or rubs.

Abdomen
Normoactive bowel sounds in all 4 quadrants. There is mild left periumbilical tenderness to palpation, mild hepatomegaly, and increased stool burden in colon.

RESULTS

CT of Abdomen, 01/23/2020.
Impression: Normal CT of abdomen.

ASSESSMENT

• Constipation
• Mild hepatomegaly

PLAN

Patrick Allen is a 42-year-old male who presents for a new patient visit for chronic abdominal pain, constipation, and diarrhea. The most likely etiology of his abdominal pain is constipation given his history, exam with increased stool burden, and normal abdominal CT. Mild hepatomegaly was also noted on exam today and the patient reportedly had elevated liver enzymes on labs with his primary care provider 4 months ago. The patient is currently on Crestor which may be contributing to his elevated liver enzymes. We discussed findings, diagnosis, and next steps at length.

Constipation
• Start daily fiber supplement and increase water consumption to at least 48 ounces daily to help with bowel regularity.
• Avoid trigger foods that may cause episodes of diarrhea.

Mild Hepatomegaly
• Repeat liver enzyme labs today.
• Schedule a liver ultrasound pending lab results.
• Encouraged to cease alcohol consumption.
• Patient to contact his PCP to discuss alternative medications.

INSTRUCTIONS

Return to clinic in 4 weeks.

section_miscategorizations.text: The doctor feels a slight enlargement of the liver on the upper right side.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This describes a physical exam finding by the clinician, not a patient-reported symptom or history.

section_miscategorizations.text: The doctor reports an enlarged liver.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is a clinician observation/exam result and belongs in objective findings, not HPI.
This is an objective condition identified by the clinician, not a subjective patient report.

section_miscategorizations.reason: Palpation of stool in the colon is a physical exam finding and should be in the Findings section.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient had slightly elevated liver enzymes in a previous physical exam four months ago.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This references prior objective lab results; while it can appear in HPI as historical context, it more strictly fits as prior objective data in Findings.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient had blood drawn four months ago.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is a prior diagnostic test rather than a symptom; it is more appropriately documented with objective data, though its presence in HPI is not highly misleading.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient's liver enzymes are slightly elevated.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is an interpretation/summary of lab results that is already restated in the Assessment; it can be considered either a finding or part of the diagnostic impression. Placement in Findings is acceptable but somewhat organizationally inconsistent.

section_miscategorizations.text: Order shoulder radiographs, initiate physical therapy, and continue acetaminophen as needed.
section_miscategorizations.reason: These orders pertain to shoulder evaluation and treatment, which are unrelated to the documented chief complaint of left hand pain and the liver/constipation issues discussed elsewhere. Their inclusion in this Plan without context is misleading, suggesting an active plan for a problem not addressed in the note.
